reasoning: TPC-H SF10 is I/O and CPU bound on 60M lineitem scans. DuckDB achieves 86-113ms for Q1, 78-113ms for Q3, 19-21ms for Q6. Key opportunities: (1) Morsel-driven parallel execution on 64 cores for lineitem scans (7-8x speedup potential), (2) SIMD vectorized filtering and aggregation for hot loops, (3) Zone maps to skip blocks in Q6/Q1, (4) Selective join ordering in Q3 (filter customer→orders→lineitem) to minimize intermediates.

focus_areas: Parallelism: Morsel-driven execution on 64 cores for full lineitem scan (59.9M rows). Target 10K-100K rows per morsel based on L3 cache sizing.
SIMD vectorized filtering: Vectorize l_shipdate <= filter using AVX-512 (compare 8 int32_t dates per instruction). Use branch-free predicates.
SIMD vectorized aggregation: Horizontal reductions for SUM, AVG on l_quantity, l_extendedprice, l_discount using AVX-512 (accumulate 8 values in parallel).
Partial aggregation: Each thread pre-aggregates into local hash table (only 4 unique groups: A/F, A/O, R/F, R/O). Global merge in single thread. Reduces shared memory contention.

Zone maps: Lineitem sorted by l_shipdate with zone maps. Pre-compute date threshold (1998-09-02) and skip blocks entirely where max_date <= threshold (reduces I/O by ~2% but exploits predicate structure).
Columnar load: Load only hot columns (l_shipdate, l_quantity, l_extendedprice, l_discount, l_returnflag, l_linestatus) to reduce memory bandwidth.
Dictionary decoding: l_returnflag/l_linestatus are dictionary-encoded uint8_t—avoid decoding in hot loop, use dictionary codes directly in aggregation keys.
Q1.strategy_notes: Q1 dominates execution time due to 60M row scan. Aggressive strategy: (1) Saturate all 64 cores with morsel-driven execution, keeping each core on 10K-100K row batches to fit L3 cache. (2) Vectorize the l_shipdate filter using AVX-512 (8 dates per instruction) to reduce branch misses. (3) In aggregation, use SIMD horizontal reductions (AVG/SUM) on groups 1-8 simultaneously. (4) Partial aggregation is critical: only 4 groups total, so local pre-agg per thread eliminates global synchronization. (5) Use compiled code generation (template specialization) for tight loop with unrolled aggregation. Target: 8-10x speedup via parallelism alone, 2-3x from SIMD, for total ~20-30x vs single-threaded baseline. DuckDB achieves ~100ms; target 40-50ms with aggressive parallelism.

focus_areas: Join ordering & selectivity: Apply filters first—filter customer by c_mktsegment='BUILDING' (0.22 selectivity) → build hash table (330K rows). Join with orders filtered by o_orderdate < 1995-03-15 (0.49 selectivity) → build second hash table (7.35M rows). Finally probe lineitem by l_orderkey with l_shipdate > filter (0.65 selectivity).

Hash join algorithm: For each join, build on smaller relation, probe with larger. Use compact hash tables (Swiss Table or Robin Hood) with SIMD-accelerated lookups. Pipeline: customer filter → orders hash join → orders+customer hash join → lineitem probe.

Predicate pushdown: Push all filters into table scans (c_mktsegment, o_orderdate, l_shipdate) before materialization. Dictionary encoding on c_mktsegment allows fast filter (uint8_t compare vs string).
Parallelism: Morsel-driven execution for each scan/join operator. Parallel probe phase in hash join (partition lineitem morsels, each thread probes its partition independently).

Top-K optimization: LIMIT 10 + ORDER BY revenue DESC suggests heap-based streaming aggregation. During aggregation, maintain min-heap of 10 largest revenue items. Skip sorting 1.4M groups; instead perform 10 comparisons per group.

Streaming aggregation: As lineitem matches orders/customer hash tables, emit (l_orderkey, revenue, o_orderdate, o_shippriority) tuples directly into streaming GROUP BY. No materialization of intermediate join result.
SIMD hash computation: For join keys (c_custkey, o_orderkey), compute hashes using AVX-512 parallelization of hash function over multiple keys in parallel.
Q3.strategy_notes: Q3 is join-heavy (3-way join with selective predicates). Strategy: (1) Strict filter-first approach: customer pre-filter (22% → 330K rows), orders pre-filter (49% → 7.35M rows), lineitem pre-filter (65% → 39M rows). (2) Build hash tables on customer and orders (smaller relations), probe lineitem. (3) Use streaming aggregation with Top-K heap (10 items) instead of full sort—this avoids 1.4M row sort materialization. (4) Parallelization critical for large joins: use work-stealing queue for parallel probe phase. (5) Compile hash join to specialize on key types and hash function. Target: 80-100ms (vs DuckDB's 78-113ms baseline).

focus_areas: Zone map skipping: Lineitem sorted by l_shipdate with zone maps. Date filter [1994-01-01, 1995-01-01) spans exactly 1 year. Likely spans 126 zone map blocks (1 per ~3 days). Skip blocks outside range to reduce I/O by ~98%.

Compound filter order: Apply filters in selectivity order: (1) l_shipdate >= / < (33% selectivity), (2) l_discount BETWEEN (9% selectivity), (3) l_quantity < 24 (48% selectivity). Combined: 0.33 * 0.09 * 0.48 = 1.4% match rate. Order filters to fail fast on most rows.

SIMD vectorized filtering: Vectorize compound predicates using AVX-512. Process 8 rows at a time with 3 comparisons (date, discount, quantity) per row using vector masks. Branch-free predicate logic (combine conditions with AND, use mask to filter).

Single aggregation (SUM): After filtering, single SUM operation. Use SIMD horizontal reduction (add 8 values per instruction) to accumulate final result. Parallelism: each thread sums its filtered rows into local accumulator, final merge is trivial (one SUM per thread).

Parallelism: Morsel-driven execution on 64 cores. Each thread processes 10K-100K row batch, filters in parallel, accumulates local sum. Global merge: simple addition of 64 partial sums (negligible overhead).

Memory access: Since zone maps eliminate 98% of blocks, filtered result fits in L3 cache. Memory bandwidth no longer bottleneck—CPU compute becomes limiting factor. Ensure SIMD utilization is maximized.
Compile tight loop: Template specialization for Q6-specific code: filter 8 rows at a time, accumulate in AVX-512 vector, horizontal add for each morsel.
Q6.strategy_notes: Q6 is highly selective (1.9% match) on a single table. Strategy: (1) Zone map skipping is THE key optimization—skip 98% of blocks, reducing I/O from 60M rows to ~1.1M rows. (2) Vectorize compound filters: date + discount + quantity as single SIMD operation (8 rows per instruction, 3 predicates per row = 24 conditions per instruction). (3) Parallelization: morsel-driven execution with 64 cores, each summing its filtered rows into local variable, then merge 64 partial sums (negligible cost). (4) Avoid branches in filter loop—use mask-based filtering and conditional accumulation. (5) Since result is tiny after filtering, no intermediate materialization needed. Target: 15-20ms (vs DuckDB's 19-21ms baseline), beating DuckDB requires ~20% improvement in filter+aggregation efficiency via SIMD/parallelism.
